coredata.dc:description: Here, Co9S8/S-CDs@PEG (CSCs@PEG) nanocomposites are synthesized by a two-step hydrothermal process. Firstly, sulfur-doped carbon dots (S-CDs) are prepared by using 3-mercaptopropionic acid as a precursor. A region with denser –SH group is regarded as nucleation site on S-CDs for the growth of Co9S8 to induce heterostructure. As-synthesized CSCs@PEG possess great harvest in NIR region and hyperthermia (51.5%, 1064 nm) for photothermal (PTT). Besides, hyperthermia can elevate the thermal energy of nanocomposite to assist low-energy NIR-II irradiation (1064 nm, 1.16 eV), realizing the excitation of Co9S8 (1.46 eV) and reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation for photodynamic therapy (PDT). What's more, CSCs@PEG exhibits GOx-like activity that could consume glucose to elevate H2O2 level and inhibit intracellular energy supply. In addition, they also show mimic peroxidase (POD) and catalase (CAT) characterization, and the tandem multi-nanozye activities endue the enhanced chemotherapy (CDT) and PDT. CSCs@PEG reveals the greater ROS generation and nanozyme activity compared with pure Co9S8@PEG, attributing to the efficient charge separation in heterostructure. The novel biodegradation of CSCs@PEG makes the elimination via feces and urine within 2 weeks. That is associated with PTT/PDT/CDT/starvation therapy to activate immune response for anticancer.

The XPS high-resolution spectra of (G) Co 2p, (H) S 2p, and (I) C 1s. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: NIR-II sensitive Co9S8/S-CDs@PEG nanocomposites with enhanced multi-enzyme mediated phototherapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, Co9S8/S-CDs@PEG (CSCs@PEG) nanocomposites are synthesized by a two-step hydrothermal process. Firstly, sulfur-doped carbon dots (S-CDs) are prepared by using 3-mercaptopropionic acid as a precursor. A region with denser –SH group is regarded as nucleation site on S-CDs for the growth of Co9S8 to induce heterostructure. As-synthesized CSCs@PEG possess great harvest in NIR region and hyperthermia (51.5%, 1064 nm) for photothermal (PTT). Besides, hyperthermia can elevate the thermal energy of nanocomposite to assist low-energy NIR-II irradiation (1064 nm, 1.16 eV), realizing the excitation of Co9S8 (1.46 eV) and reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation for photodynamic therapy (PDT). What's more, CSCs@PEG exhibits GOx-like activity that could consume glucose to elevate H2O2 level and inhibit intracellular energy supply. In addition, they also show mimic peroxidase (POD) and catalase (CAT) characterization, and the tandem multi-nanozye activities endue the enhanced chemotherapy (CDT) and PDT. CSCs@PEG reveals the greater ROS generation and nanozyme activity compared with pure Co9S8@PEG, attributing to the efficient charge separation in heterostructure. The novel biodegradation of CSCs@PEG makes the elimination via feces and urine within 2 weeks. That is associated with PTT/PDT/CDT/starvation therapy to activate immune response for anticancer.

ce:section.ce:para: Phototherapy including photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT) has attracted most attention on anticancer owing to many advantages, such as: non-invasiveness, spatial and temporal controllability, and low toxic side effects [1] etc. Phototherapy employs appropriate photosensitizers, which can convert near infrared (NIR) irradiation into reactive oxygen species (ROS) and hyperthermia to cause irreversible cellular damage and apoptosis [2,3]. NIR-II light (1000–1700 nm), with the greater tissue penetration (3 cm) and higher skin-tolerance threshold (1 W cm−2), is more suitable for deep cancer therapy [4,5]. However, NIR-II light possesses too low energy to stimulate photosensitizer to generate ROS, directly. The combination of upconversion nanomaterials has been adopted to realize NIR-II excitation, while low quantum yield and complicated procedure inhibit the further application [6,7]. Therefore, the preparation of simple nanocomposite with simultaneous and synergistic PDT and PTT triggered by single NIR-II laser would have great significance.

In addition, highly complex tumor microenvironment (TME) has developed low O2 concentration (pO2 ≤ 2.5 mmHg) [8]. Tumor hypoxia not only reduces PDT effects (O2-dependent ROS generation) but also benefits for tumor proliferation and metastasis [9,10]. Recently, mimic catalase (CAT) has been explored to convert endogenous H2O2 into O2 to alleviate hypoxia and enhance PDT. Given the limited H2O2 concentration (50–100 μM) in TME, extra H2O2 supplementation is desired for anticancer [11,12]. Owing to Warburg effect, the concentration of glucose in tumor cells is higher than that in normal cells, endowing glucose oxidase (GOx) with potential to oxidize intratumoral glucose into H2O2 [13,14]. It is known that glucose plays an important role in the rapid growth and reproduction of tumor by metabolizing ATP to supply energy. The consumption of glucose could significantly inhibit tumor growth, achieving starving therapy [15]. Chang et al. synthesized CuCl-CDs to reveal mimic-POD activity, which was combined with polydopamine (PDA) and glucose oxidase (GOx) to achieve starvation therapy and enhanced cellular H2O2 levels for CDT [16]. Wong et al. constructed Ce6/GOx@ZIF-8/PDA@MnO2 nanocomposite to achieve enzymatic enhancement of PDT [17]. However, GOx suffers the inherent disadvantages of natural enzymes, such as: high cost and easy inactivation. Au nanoparticles (NPs) have been found to be excellent mimic-GOx, and Au-based nanomaterials are widely in oxidation of glucose for bio-detection and bio-medicine [18]. Given the expensive preparation of noble metals, some non-noble metal oxides also were developed as GOx-mimics. Li et al. synthesized MnO2 nanoﬂakes with GOx-like activity and peroxidase-like activity for colorimetric glucose sensing [19]. V2O5 nanobelts were synthesized to show GOx-like property for non-enzymatic online optical glucose measurement [20]. Shen et al. also discovered the mimic GOx activity of CoxOyHz for glucose determination [21]. Based on the previous reports, some Co-based materials were employed on nonenzymatic electrochemical glucose sensors [22,23]. In addition, Co ions also possess Fenton catalytic activity therefore Co-based materials are expected to reveal tandem nanozyme activity, promoting chemotherapy (CDT) and PDT. However, there is not related report.

In this paper, Co9S8/S-CDs@PEG (CSCs@PEG) nanoheterostructures are constructed to integrate NIR-II sensitive phototherapy and multi-nanozyme activity, facilitating anticancer efficiency. Firstly, sulfur-doped carbon dots (S-CDs) are obtained by using 3-mercaptopropionic acid as carbon and sulfur source. It is believed that –SH groups are randomly distributed on the surface of carbon dots. A region with denser –SH groups can be served as active site for the nucleation of Co9S8 that dominates the synthesis of heterostructure finally. As-synthesized CSC@PEGs reveal great harvest in NIR-II region and photothermal conversion (CSC-2@PEG, 51.5 %) under 1064 nm light irradiation. In addition, hyperthermia also fills up the energy difference between incident light (1064 nm, 1.16 eV) and band gap of Co9S8 (1.46 eV), making NIR-II excited PTT and PDT, simultaneously. What's more, CSCs@PEG exhibits GOx-like activity that not only reduces intracellular energy supply but also increases H2O2 content. Besides, they also show peroxidase (POD), CAT, and GSH oxidase (GSHOD) mimics activity. The tandem multi-nanozyme activities could introduce starvation therapy and enhanced CDT and PDT. It can be found that CSCs@PEG possess the greater ROS generation and nanozyme activity than that of pure Co9S8@PEG. In addition, biodegradation performance also is investigated, showing the quick degradation and elimination within 2 weeks. The integration of PTT, PDT, CDT, and starvation therapy into one nanocomposite arouses anti-tumor immune responses. Both in vitro and vivo experiments reveal the great anticancer effect of CSCs@PEG.

All requires chemicals were used of analytical grade and used without further purification. 3-Mercaptopropionic acid, SH-PEG-SH, Dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), and 4, 6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) were purchased from Aladdin Co. Ltd. Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), 3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), Hydrogen dioxide (H2O2), 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2-Htetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Propidium iodide (PI) was purchased from Alfa Aesar Co. Ltd. Calcein acetoxymethylester (calcein AM) was supplied by KeyGENBioTCH. Fluorescein 5(6)-isothiocyanate (FITC) was from Macklin.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were tested by Hitachi H8100 at an accelerating voltage of 20 kV. Powder X-ray patterns (XRD) were tested on RigakuULtima IV diffractometer with Cu Ka radiation (40 kV, 20 mA). UV–Vis spectra were recorded on SHIMADZU UV2550 spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra were surveyed by HORIBA FL-3. The MRI images in vitro and vivo were obtained from BioSpec 94/20 USR (Bruker).

50 mg of 3-mercaptopropionic acid was added to 20 mL of mixed solution of water and ethanol (1:1 by volume), and the pH value of solution was adjusted to about 8 using sodium hydroxide solution. After that, the solution was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave at 200 °C for 12 h to obtain sulfur-carbon dot solution.

Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (48.5/72.75/145.5 mg) was added to the sulfur-doped carbon dots solution (20 mL) and stirred to form a homogeneous solution, transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave at 200 °C for 12 h. The reactants were cooled to room temperature, washed several times with deionized water, and freeze-dried to obtain product CSC-1, CSC-2 and CSC-3, respectively.

50 mg of 3-mercaptopropionic acid was added to 20 mL of mixed water and ethanol solution (1:1 by volume). Some sodium hydroxide solution was added until pH = 8. And then, 48.5 mg of cobalt nitrate hexahydrate was put in with stirring until a homogeneous solution was formed. After the further reaction at 200 °C for 12 h, the solution was centrifuged, washed several times with deionized water and freeze-dried to obtain Y-CSC-2.

50 mg of sodium sulfide was added to 20 mL of a mixed solution of water (pH = 8) and ethanol (1:1 by volume), and then the solution was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave at 200 °C for 12 h. The reaction product was collected by centrifugation, washed several times with deionized water, and freeze-dried to obtain Co9S8 nanoparticles.

The as-prepared sample was added into water to form dispersion solution (200 μg mL −1). Then 20 mg of SH-PEG-SH was added. After the stirring for 6 h, the sample was obtained by centrifugation and washed twice with water and freeze-dried.

Sample dispersion of 1 mL at different concentrations (12.5–100 μg mL−1) were exposed to 1064 nm irradiation at different power densities (0.5–2.0 W cm−2) for 10 min. Temperatures were recorded every minute using FLIR infrared thermal imager E8.

100 μL of ethanol solution containing 3 mg of 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) was added to CSC-2@PEG dispersion (100 μg mL−1, 1 mL, PBS pH = 7.4) followed by exposure to 1064 nm irradiation (0.8 W cm−2). The mixture was centrifuged and the supernatant was collected and analyzed by fluorescence spectroscopy.

ESR (electron spin resonance) was used to confirm ⋅O2 − and ⋅OH radical by using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as spin trapping agent. Firstly, 200 mM of trapping agent and CSC-2@PEG solution (10 mg mL−1) were mixed (1:1 by volume), and then transferred to ESR tube immediately and illuminated under 1064-nm laser for 10 min.

CSC-2@PEG dispersion (1 mg mL−1), TMB (40 mM), H2O2 (10 mM) or glucose solution (10 mM) were added to 1 mL of pH = 4.0 NaAC-HAC buffer solution, incubated at 37 °C for 10 min. After centrifugation, the supernatant was taken for UV–Visible detection and the change in absorbance at 652 nm was recorded indicating the formation of oxTMB.

GSH depletion was detected using UV–Visible spectroscopy. Different concentrations of CSC-2@PEG dispersions (0, 31.25, 62.5, 125, 250, 500 μg mL −1) were mixed with certain concentrations of GSH and incubated for 20 min at 37 °C. And then, 100 μL of the above mixture was added to 800 μL of PBS, and 100 μL of 5,5-dithiodibenzoic acid (DTNB) solution (10 mg mL−1) was added. After centrifugation to remove the material, the absorbance of supernatant was measured by UV–Visible spectrometry.

Ammonium molybdate can react with H2O2 to form a yellow solution. 400 μL of ammonium molybdate (100 mM), 20 μL of H2O2 (10 mM), and 100 μL of CSC-2@PEG dispersion (100 μg mL−1) were added in NaAC-HAC buffer solution (pH = 4.0), and incubated for different times at 37 °C. And, the UV–Vis absorption curve of supernatant was recorded.

A certain amount of silver nitrate solution was added to 5 mL of CSC-2@PEG dispersion (100 μg mL−1) and illuminated for different times. The amount of oxygen change was recorded by an oxygen dissolution meter.
An amount of H2O2 (100 mM) was added to 5 mL of CSC-2@PEG dispersion (100 μg mL−1) under slight stirring, and the amount of oxygen was recorded in real time using oxygen dissolution meter.

4T1 (mouse breast cancer) and L929 (fibroblast cell line) cells monomolecular layers were cultured in RPMI1640 or DMEM medium containing 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin (100 U mL−1) and streptomycin (100 μg mL−1) at 37 °C and 5% CO2 in a humidified environment.

To test in vitro cytotoxicity, the cell viability was assayed by MTT method. Firstly, cells were cultured in 96-well plates at the density of 8 × 103 per well for 24 h. Then, different concentrations of CSC-2@PEG were co-cultured for another 24 h. After that, the medium was taken off to remove the cells without uptake, and new medium was put in. These cells were irradiated under 1064 nm laser for 15 min or without laser before the cells were further incubated in medium containing 0.5 mg mL−1 MTT for 4 h. Finally, the waste solution was discarded and 150 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added to each well. The absorbance of the DMSO solution was detected by an enzyme marker WD-2102A at a wavelength of 492 nm. Cytotoxicity was expressed as the percentage of cell survival in each group relative to the cell survival in the control group.

4T1 cells were seeded in single-well plates at a density of 1 × 10 5 cells per well and incubated for 24 h. CSC-2@PEG NPs (50 μg mL −1) were added and incubated for another 12 h. The intracellular ATP concentration was measured with an ATP assay kit (Beyotime Biotechnology).
The following fluorescence imaging was performed on Leica DFC450C Microsystems Ltd:

AM/PI imaging: 4T1 cells were incubated in single-well plates at 1 × 105–1.5 × 105 numbers per well for 24 h. And different concentrations of CSC-2@PEG NPs (0, 30, 60 μg mL −1) were added to co-incubate for 12 h. The cells were illuminated with 1064 nm light at different power densities, and then stained with calcein acetoxymethyl ester (calcein-AM) and propidium iodide (PI) for 30 min, respectively. Finally, the excess dye was washed off with PBS for imaging analysis.

To assess material uptake, CSC-2@PEG NPs (50 μg mL −1) was co-incubated with 4T1 cells for 0.5, 1 and 3 h, and 3 h + NIR. DAPI was used for nuclear staining. After staining, cells were washed three times with PBS, and then imaging acquisition was performed.

To evaluate intracellular O2 and ROS production, 4T1 cells were incubated with CSC-2@PEG (50 μg mL−1) for 12 h, after that each well was washed three times with PBS. Cells were irradiated with 1064 nm laser (0.8 W cm−2) for 5 min then they were incubated with RDPP (5 μmol mL−1) for 20 min, washed three times with PBS, followed by the addition of DCFH-DA (10 μmol mL−1) and continued incubation for 10 min in incubator. Finally the plates were washed three times with PBS, and the fluorescence images were collected.

To assess mitochondrial membrane potential damage, 4T1 cells were incubated at 1–1.5 × 105 per well in single-well plates for 24 h. These cells were then co-cultured with CSC-2@PEG NPs (50 μg mL −1) for another 12 h, followed by irradiation under 1064 nm laser (0.8 W cm−2) for 10 min. These cells were stained with JC-1 (5 μg mL −1) for 30 min, followed by washing with PBS three times, and finally for imaging acquisition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and vivo T 2-weighted MR imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The dispersions with various concentrations of CSC-2@PEG were prepared to obtain MR images and signal strength. After scanning, the r 2 relaxivity value was obtained by the curve fitting of 1/T 2 relaxation time (s−1) versus Co concentration (mg mL−1). In vivo MR images were obtained from tumor-bearing mice before and after injection with CSC-2@PEG suspension (100 μL).

4T1 cells were implanted under the left buttock of female Balb/c mice to establish the tumor model. All the mouse experiments were performed in compliance with the criteria of The National Regulation of China for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals. The tumor-bearing mice were randomly divided into 4 groups: (1) Saline (control group), (2) NIR-II, (3) CSC-2@PEG, (4) CSC-2@PEG + NIR-II. Each douse of CSC-2@PEG was 4 mg kg −1. After tail intravenous injection, group (2) and (4) were irradiated with 1064 nm laser (0.8 W cm −2) for 10 min and FLIR infrared thermal imager E8 was used to monitor temperature changes. After different treatments, the tumor volume and body weight of each group was measured every two days. After treatment for 12, 72, and 168 h, mice were sacrificed for harvesting blood and then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min to obtain serum. The content of TNF-α, IFN-γ, and IL-2 in serum was measured using ELISA kit (Dakewe Biotech Co., Ltd.).

After treatment for 14 d, heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, and tumor tissue in control and CSC-2@PEG + NIR group were employed for histological analysis. After a series of treatments, the encased tissues were sliced and stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). And the slices were observed by optical microscope.
Blood sample was taken from the eyes of mice on 14th day for blood analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flow cytometry assay for immune response
ce:section.ce:para: To study the maturation of dendritic cells, inguinal lymph nodes were harvested from mice of different treatment groups and steeped in a glass homogenizer containing 2% heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (pH 7.4). Single cell suspensions were prepared by grinding, washed with PBS (pH 7.4), and filtered through a 200-mesh cell sieve. The remaining cells were centrifuged for 10 min. Cells from inguinal lymph nodes were then stained with anti-CD11cAPC, anti-mouse CD80FITC and anti-mouse CD86 PE.

In order to prepare nanoheterostrcture, a two-step synthetic strategy was carried out. Firstly, sulfur-doped carbon dots (S-CDs) are synthesized via hydrothermal method using 3-mercaptopropionic acid as carbon source. According to TEM image, as-synthesized S-CDs are about 5 nm in size with the interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to (100) plane of graphite carbon as present in Fig. S1. And then, cobalt nitrate is added into S-CDs solution to undergo another hydrothermal treatment to obtain Co9S8/S-CDs (CSCs) nanocomposite (Scheme 1 ). From Fig. 1 A and Fig. S2, CSC-2 reveals nanoparticles about 10 nm in size. By varying the amount of cobalt salt, CSC-1, CSC-2, and CSC-3 are prepared (Table S1). As displaced in Fig. 1F, all samples reveal the characteristic diffraction peaks of Co9S8 (JCPDS NoPDF: 02–1459), which are gradually enhanced with the increase of cobalt amount. However, there is no obvious diffraction of carbon owing to the low crystallinity of S-CDs. HRTEM image in Fig. 1B and C further discovers the obvious lattice fringe of carbon (d100 = 0.21 nm) and Co9S8 (d222 = 0.28 nm) are focused at the two sides of nanocomposite, demonstrating the heterostructure of Co9S8/S-CDs. XPS analysis is used to further probe the component and valence of CSC-2 nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. 1G, the peaks of Co 2p spectrum could be fitted to Co2+ (797.3 and 781.5 eV) and Co3+ (778.1 and 793.5 eV) peaks. In S 2p spectrum, the bonding energy of 162.0 and 161.0 eV is corresponding to S2 2− and S2−, respectively (Fig. 1H). In addition, the signal at 163.5 eV is attributed to C–S and S–O bond at 168.5 eV also can be found derived from surface oxidation. In C 1s region, the peak at 284.6 eV corresponds to CC/C–C bond of graphite carbon component (Fig. 1I). And the signals at 288.6 and 285.7 eV are assigned to CO and C–S bond. These data confirm that the heterostructure of Co9S8 and S-CDs is synthesized successfully.

In view of above, under hydrothermal condition, the carbonization of 3-mercaptopropionic acid produces carbon dots with abundant –SH and –COOH on surface, making the negative zeta potential of S-CDs (−26.25 ± 2.10 mV). It is known that interaction between -S- and Co2+ is more than that of -COO- and Co2+ based on hard and soft acid-base theory. Therefore, a region with denser –SH groups would capture more free Co2+ and is served as nucleation site on S-CDs. Once crystal nucleus is formed, the growth of cobalt sulfide is more likely to take place rather than the formation of new nucleus, ascribing to the energy barrier of nucleation. After the growth of Co9S8, CSC-2 reveal the increased zeta potential (−14.69 ± 1.4 mV) due to the decrease of surface –SH (Fig. S3). To further survey the formation of nanostructure, one-pot synthetic process is carried out for comparison, where 3-mercaptopropionic acid and cobalt nitrate are put in simultaneously via a hydrothermal process to obtain Y-CSC-2 (Fig. S4B). XRD pattern also discovers Co9S8 crystal phase (Fig. S4A). EDX analysis further discovers its lower carbon content (32.70 %) than that of CSC-2 (49.31 %) that is consistent with TG analysis as shown in Fig. S5. It is believed that pre-formed S-CDs are significant for nanoheterostructure formation. In addition, if S-CDs solution is dialyzed, no Co9S8 is formed, indicating that the residual S component in S-CDs solution also take part in sulfuration. Here, 3-mercaptopropionic acid is carefully selected to be carbon resource as well as sulfur resource for the production of CSC heterostructure.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra are surveyed as shown in Fig. 2 A. All samples have great absorption in whole spectrum as well as NIR-II region, and the absorbance increases with sample concentration (Fig. S6A). All CSCs@PEG samples reveal a higher harvest than pure Co9S8@PEG. With appropriate Co amount, CSC-2@PEG exhibits the highest NIR-II harvest of all. Their photothermal performances are tested under 1064 nm laser irradiation (0.8 W cm−2). From Fig. 2B, the temperatures of all samples increase after irradiation is introduced. With the greatest NIR-II adsorption, CSC-2@PEG exhibits the highest temperature increment (26.5 °C) within 10 min while that of water only rises 7.2 °C at the same condition. And the photothermal conversion efficiency of CSC-2@PEG is calculated to be 51.5 % (Figs. S6B–C) that is higher than many reported materials, including FeCo@carbon nanoparticles (η = 26.7 %) [24], chitosan-5Fu-graphitic carbon nanocages (η = 23.7 %) [25], CoP NPs (η = 25.7 %) [26], Mn–CuS@BSA NDs (η = 40.07 %) [27] etc. In addition, the photothermal ability of CSC-2@PEG is dependent on sample concentration (Fig. 2C) and illumination power (Fig. 2D) which also are confirmed from thermal imaging (Figs. 2E and S6D). To explore the photothermal stability of CSC-2@PEG, the five on-off cycles of NIR-II irradiation are performed. From Fig. 2F, temperature loss during these cycles is negligible, exhibiting good photothermal stability. The great photothermal ability combined with adjustable performance and novel photothermal stability endows CSC-2@PEG with potential application in PTT.

To evaluate ROS generation, 2′,7′-dichloroﬂuorescein (DCFH) is used as a detector, which can be oxidized by ROS to form a green fluorescent DCF with characteristic peak around 520 nm. From Fig. 2G, all samples reveal obvious emission peaks, implying ROS production under NIR-II irradiation. Furthermore, CSCs@PEG composites show more ROS than pure Co9S8@PEG. Among them, CSC-2@PEG reveals the greatest ROS generation. From Fig. S7, ROS production is dependent on irradiation time, implying controllable PDT performance in further application. To distinct the active resource of generated ROS, isopropyl alcohol (IPA) and p-benzoquinone (BPQ) are used as ⋅OH and ⋅O2 − shielding agents. From Fig. 2H, ROS production decreases significantly with the addition of IPA and BPQ, indicating that ⋅OH and ⋅O2 − are the active radicals of ROS. Besides, ESR spectrum also is collected to provide direct evidence by using 5,5-dimethly-1-proline-N-oxide (DMPO) as indicator. As shown in Fig. 2I, both ⋅OH and ⋅O2 − signals can be found in ESR spectra which is consistent with capturing experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The possible mechanism of NIR-II sensitive ROS generation
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to investigate the possible mechanism of ROS generation, the energy level structure of sample is explored. Firstly, the band gap of Co9S8 is determined by UV diffuse reflectance spectrum to be 1.46 eV as displayed in Fig. S8A. And then, Fermi level (Ef) of Co9S8 (−0.94 V vs. Ag/AgCl) is detected by using flat band potential (Efp) from Mott–Schottky (M − S) plot (Fig. S8B). Moreover, the positive slope of M − S plot demonstrates n-type characterization of Co9S8. In view of the previous reports, the position of conduction band potential (ECB) is always 0.1–0.2 eV higher than that of Ef for n-type semiconductor [28]. Therefore, CB and VB potential can be calculated to be −0.94 and 0.52 eV (vs. NHE) for Co9S8. The heterostructure is constructed as shown in Fig. 3 A. When NIR-II laser is turned on, it cannot induce the separation of electrons and holes for Co9S8 because the energy of 1064 nm laser is lower than the band gap of Co9S8. Based on above investigation, the photothermal effect of CSC-2 can convert partial incident light into heat, elevating the thermal energy. When the sum of heat energy and light energy exceeds the band gap of Co9S8, electrons can be excited into CB, remaining holes in VB. The electrons possess enough reducing capacity (ECB = −0.94 eV) to capture dissolved O2 to form ⋅O2 − (EO2/⋅O2 − = −0.33 eV) according to Eqn. (1). What's more, these electrons and dissolved O2 also can undergo another pathway (Eqn. (2)), inducing ⋅OH (EO2/HO2⋅ = −0.13 eV).

To further confirm the above deduction, methanol is added as a hole-trapper which promotes ROS generation because the capture of hole is favor of the gathering of electrons onto CB. When dissolved O2 is removed by Ar bubbles, ROS production also decreases. On account of above investigation, it is electron and dissolved O2 to determine ROS generation. Therefore, PDT can be defined as photo-catalytic oxidation in which the produced ROS oxidize intracellular biomolecules to induce apoptosis.

As exhibited in Fig. 2G, CSCs heterostructures exhibit more ROS generation than pure Co9S8 owing to the combination of S-CDs component. With great conduction ability, carbon materials always are used for electron transfer, facilitating the separation of photo-generated electrons and holes [29,30]. From Fig. 3B, the electrons in CB of Co9S8 would transform onto S-CDs and effective charge separation is further verified by the greater transient photocurrent and lower radius in electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) (Fig. 3C and D). In addition, Y-CSC-2 (one-pot sample), which also is consistent of carbon and Co9S8, reveals the lower ROS generation because of its non-heterostructure (Fig. 3E). From Fig. 3B, hyperpyrexia derived from photothermal effect is significant for the generation of photo-exited charges. Here, the cooling condition is adopted to shield hyperpyrexia. From Fig. 3F–C, the decreased ROS and photocurrent under cooling condition further testifies that hyperpyrexia real can assist the generation of photo-excited electrons and ROS production as well. With low energy, it is difficult to realize NIR–II–triggered PDT. The combination of photothermal component is a feasible method to elevate the thermal energy of sample that would efficiently decrease the energy requirement from excited light. Based on the above investigation, it is photothermal effect and novel heterostructure to decide the great NIR–II–sensitive ROS generation. In addition, intracellular NIR-II ROS production is surveyed with DCFH-DA as indicator. As shown in Fig. 3G, control and NIR-II groups show no obvious green fluorescence. Some green fluorescence in CSC-2@PEG group is derived from Fenton-like action that would be discussed in the next part. The conspicuous green fluorescence in CSC-2@PEG + NIR-II group further verify the intracellular ROS generation for PDT.

Currently, various mimic-nanozyme have been adopted in anticancer with great efficiency. Firstly, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethyldiphenylamine (TMB) is used to detect ⋅OH from CSC-2@PEG and H2O2 solution because TMB can be oxidized by ⋅OH to form blue oxTMB with a characteristic absorption at 652 nm. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the obvious adsorption peak and blue solution demonstrates the Fenton-like behavior of CSC-2@PEG, which can react with H2O2 to produce ⋅OH. Based on Eqn. (3), Co2+ can be oxidized by H2O2 to form Co3+ and ⋅OH. And Co3+ also go on react with H2O2, inducing O2. From Fig. 4B, the increased dissolved oxygen content verifies the reaction according to Eqn. (4). It is redox cycle between Co2+ and Co3+ to insure the conversion of H2O2 into ·OH and O2 incessantly. The performance can be regarded as mimic peroxidase (POD) and catalase (CAT) activity. In addition, H2O2-consuming also is verified by ammonium molybdate assay as exhibited in Fig. S9.

Moreover, it is can be found that CSC-2@PEG reveals a higher activity than pure Co9S8@PEG and that is further studied by enzyme kinetic theory. Michaelis-Menten curves are obtained by varying H2O2 concentration and fitted by Lineweaver-Burk equation. As shown in Fig. S10, CSC-2@PEG display a higher maximum initial velocity (2.5 × 10−8 M s −1) and lower Michaelis constant (0.17 mM) compared with Co9S8@PEG (2.1 × 10−8 M s−1, 0.18 mM), indicating the stronger affinity and enzymatic activity of CSC-2@PEG toward H2O2. From Fig. S12, the shift of bonding energy in Co 2p spectrum manifests the electron transfer between Co9S8 and S-CDs. And the lower resistance of charge transfer in heterostructure is responsible for a greater nanoenzyme activity of CSC-2@PEG (Figs. 3D and S13). The anisotropic architectures always possess the greater catalytic properties than monocomponent or their physical mixtures [31]. In addition, under NIR-II irradiation, more ⋅OH and O2 generation can be found from Fig. 4A and B. In fact, CSC-2@PEG also can induce ⋅OH under NIR-II illumination so that it is difficult to distinguish them. The increase of O2 evolution under irradiation is ascribed to photothermal effect which enhances CAT activity, and more O2 supplementation can make for ROS generation, facilitating PDT.

To further demonstrate POD and CAT activity in vitro, CSC-2@PEG is incubated with 4T1 cells and intracellular hypoxia level and ROS production are evaluated using [Ru(dpp)3]Cl2 (RDPP) and DCFH-DA as probe, respectively. As exhibited in Fig. 4C, red fluorescence (hypoxia level) in CSC-2@PEG group significantly weakens with some green fluorescence (ROS production) lighting up in comparison with control group due to the CAT and POD activity of CSC-2@PEG. After irradiation for 5 min, hypoxia is further relieved and more ROS generation can be found that is consistent with above investigation. Moreover, the immunostaining of hypoxia-inducible factor-1α (HIF-1α) in vivo is also carried out with DBA as colorant. From Fig. 4D, intratumor HIF-1α level drops prominently in comparison with control group, owing to O2 supplement from CAT activity.

It is known that in order to balance the intracellular levels of oxidative stress, a high concentration of GSH (10 mM) is also present in tumor cells to maintain redox homeostasis. To explore the effect of reductive GSH on POD activity, TMB was used by varying the concentrations of GSH (0–10 mM). As revealed in Fig. 4E, when 1 mM of GSH is added, ⋅OH generation increases instead. According to Eqn. (5), GSH facilitates the conversion from Co3+ to Co2+, which further catalyzes H2O2 to form ⋅OH. However, go on increasing the concentration of GSH, ⋅OH generation decreases due to the reduction of excess GSH. Moreover, the consumption of GSH is further confirmed by using 5,5-dithiodibenzoic acid (2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) as indicator, which reacts with GSH to produce a yellow product. From Fig. 4F, with increasing concentration of CSC-2@PEG, the content of GSH decreases obviously. The performance can be defined as GSH oxidase (GSHOD) activity of CSC-2@PEG that is significant to disturb intratumor redox balance, boosting oxidative stress for anticancer.

In addition to the above nanoenzyme behaviors, it is found that the combination of glucose and CSC-2@PEG also can induce the oxidation of TMB. As shown in Fig. 4G, the peak of ox-TMB enhances after extra glucose is added. It is known that glucose can be converted into H2O2 and gluconic acid in the presence of glucose oxidase (Gox). Whether CSC-2@PEG can be considered as mimic Gox? From Fig. 4H, the absorption of KMnO4 solution decreases significantly after treatment with CSC-2@PEG + glucose, further demonstrating H2O2 production. In addition, the pH value of CSC-2@PEG solution with or without glucose is recorded. As shown in Fig. 4I, the addition of glucose decreases pH value from 7.0 to 5.2. Without glucose, there is negligible change of acidity. In order to further discover the catalysis process of glucose, cyclic voltammetry (CV) is employed, showing the obvious oxidation peak after the addition of glucose (Fig. 4J). On account of above investigation, it is the mimic Gox activity of CSC-2@PEG to convert glucose, forming gluconic acid and H2O2 to elevate acidity. And the generated H2O2 can be transformed in-situ to form ⋅OH owing to the POD activity of CSC-2@PEG as shown in Fig. 4K. In addition, tandem Gox and POD performance is further studied in vitro. As displayed in Fig. 4J, pure glucose cannot induce intracellular ROS compared with control group. The enhanced green fluorescence for glucose + CSC-2@PEG group is attributed to supplemental H2O2 (mimic-Gox) and in-situ conversion (mimic-POD). Compared with previous mimic Gox (Au, Ag, and Pt), non-noble metal nanozyme (CSC-2@PEG) possesses great potential in practical application [32,33]. However, there is little report about this. Based on above, CSC-2@PEG integrates multi-nanoenzyme activity (CAT, POD, GSHOD, and Gox) to adjust tumor microenvironment for anticancer.

PEG was grafted outside in the hope to improve the dispersion stability of nanocomposite. After storage for 2 days, CSC-2@PEG still retains favorable dispersibility in various media, including water, PBS, DMEM and FBS (Fig. S14). To track uptake by 4T1 cells, fluorescent FITC is modified onto CSC-2@PEG. As shown in Fig. S15, some green fluorescence could be found in cytoplasm after 0.5 h of incubation. The quick uptake thanks to the small nanoparticle size and great cell adhesion of CSC-2@PEG. As incubation time increases, more CSC-2@PEG nanoparticles entered into cytoplasm. Intracellular green fluorescence is improved significantly under NIR-II irradiation due to hyperpyrexia that accelerates cell phagocytosis and cytokinesis (Fig. S15). To visualize the synergistic therapeutic effect of CSC-2@PEG in vitro, AM (green) and PI (red) are used as indicators to stain live and dead cells, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5 A, with the increase of irradiation intensities or material concentrations, green fluorescence attenuates with red fluorescence enhancing, indicating apoptosis derived from the synergistic effect of phototherapy and nanozyme.

Moreover, MTT assay is adopted to quantitate cytotoxicity of CSC-2@PEG and NIR-II. As shown in Fig. 5B, after incubation for 12 h, the viability of 4T1 cells drops to 56.18 ± 2.61 % (300 μg mL−1) that further declines to 36.42 ± 2.54 % (300 μg mL−1) when incubation time extends to 48 h. The sample concentration/incubation time-dependent cytotoxicity is ascribed to the tandem Gox and POD activity of CSC-2@PEG. Moreover, normal L929 cell is used as contrast. Even when sample concentration increases to 300 μg mL−1, survival rate could still remain 92.40 ± 2.82 %, showing novel biocompatibility. The distinct cytotoxicity for normal and cancer cell is attributed to overexpressed H2O2 and high concentration glucose in cancer cells that ensure the specific cytotoxicity for anticancer. As shown in Fig. 5C, when extra H2O2 and glucose is added, the viability of 4T1 cell decreases from 66.83 ± 3.22 % to 58.58 ± 2.16 % (H2O2), 51.85 ± 1.98 % (glucose), 40.62 ± 1.97 % (glucose + H2O2), further verifying the tandem Gox and POD activity for high cytotoxicity.

It is well known that during glucose metabolism, adenosine 5′-triphosphate (ATP) and other biological components is produced in tumor cells to supply energy for rapid cell growth. Therefore, cutting off the energy supply of ATP by consuming glucose could introduce starvation therapy [34]. Here, intracellular ATP level is detected after treatment with CSC-2@PEG for different times. As shown in Fig. 5D, intracellular ATP level decreases significantly compared to control group and that also reveals sample-concentration dependence. The consumption of intracellular glucose by CSC-2@PEG restrains ATP energy supply to achieve starvation therapy. From Fig. 5E, under NIR-II irradiation, cell survival rate further decreases to 11.56 ± 3.14% (50 μg mL−1) at 24 h incubation, owing to the synergistic effect of PTT, PDT, CDT, and starvation therapy. To further investigate the mechanism of apoptosis, the damage of mitochondria is evaluated by using JC-1 as probe to reveal the change of mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP). As shown in Fig. 5F, control group reveals bright red fluorescence is derived from JC aggregation with normal MMP. Pure CSC-2@PEG group exhibits some green fluorescence of JC monomer, indicating the decreased MMP and damaged mitochondrial membrane caused by CDT and starvation. CSC-2@PEG + NIR-II reveals strongly green fluorescence, proving the major damage of mitochondrial membrane derived from synergistic therapy. It is hyperthermia, ROS, and starvation from CSC-2@PEG + NIR-II to destroy mitochondria and led to apoptosis eventually.

The biodegradation and metabolism behavior is significant for the practical applications of nano-reagents. The degradation process of CSC-2@PEG is studied under different PBS (pH 5.0 and 7.4) at 37 °C. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the color of dispersion turns from dark black to light gradually. It also can be found that the color change at neutral condition is quicker than that at weak acidic condition. And the change also can be reflected from UV–Vis absorption spectra of CSC-2@PEG during this process. From Fig. 6B and C, absorption decrease by half at pH 7.4 after 12 h, while it takes 3 d to reach similar degradation at pH 5.0. The particular degradation behavior is favor of appropriate residence at tumor region (weak acidic condition) and the quick elimination from other normal tissues (neutral condition).

In order to further understand the degradation process of CSC-2@PEG, TEM analysis is adopted to reveal gradual change (Fig. 6D and E). After 7 d, these particles collapse below 2 nm in size. From Fig. 6F, the diffraction peaks of CSC-2@PEG disappear after degradation. From S XPS spectrum (Fig. 6G), S–O band is enhanced obviously after degradation with the percentage increasing from 16.63% to 29.46% (Table S2). Co 3+ content also increases as shown in Fig. 6H and Table S2. In addition, degradation and metabolism behavior also is studied in vivo. CSC-2@PEG suspension is injected into tumor-bearing mice via tail vein, and then feces and urine of mice are collected and analyzed by ICP. As shown in Fig. 6I, a large amount of Co ions are detected in feces and urine after injection. In view of above, it is the oxidation of S2− to transform insoluble sulfide into soluble sulfate, dominating biodegradation [35,36]. The collapsed nanostructure with smaller particle size facilitates elimination via feces and urine.

Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), a non-invasive imaging technique, can accurately determine the location and size of tumors. In this paper, both Co2+ (3d [7]) and Co3+ (3d [6]) ions contain unpaired 3d electrons, which endow CSC-2@PEG with the potential to be MRI contrast agent. As shown in Fig. 7 A, MR signal gradually darken with increasing sample concentration. The good linear relationship between concentration and transverse relaxation (1/T 2) can be found with a high relaxivity value (r 2) of 28.81 (pH 5.6) and 24.56 (pH 7.4) mg/mL/s (Fig. 7B). In vivo MRI behaviour is also explored using 4T1 tumor-bearing mice as model. From Fig. 7C, the colour of tumor area significantly darkens after the injection of CSC@-2@PEG. Given great photothermal effect, the thermal imaging ability of CSC-2@PEG also is studied by thermal imaging camera (E8). Under NIR-II irradiation, tumor region is visualized owing to hyperpyrexia from photothermal effect (Fig. 7D). Fig. 7E further exhibits obvious temperature increment up to 61.5 °C for CSC-2@PEG + NIR-II, while that of control group is just 45.8 °C. As shown in Fig. 7, CSC-2@PEG can be adopted as contrast agent for MR and PT imaging, and di-mode imaging ability further ensures accurate diagnosis and guides for tumor treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo treatment and immune activation
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the in vivo anticancer effect of CSC-2@PEG nanoparticles, 4T1 tumor-bearing Balb/c mice are divided into 4 groups randomly: 1) control, 2) NIR-II, 3) CSC-2@PEG, and 4) CSC-2@PEG + NIR-II group. After the intravenous injection of CSC-2@PEG, their body weights and tumor volumes are recorded every 2 days. As shown in Fig. 8 C, body weights for all groups slightly increase that proves the negligible adverse effects of laser and CSC-2@PEG during treatment. From Fig. 8D, the tumor volume of control and NIR-II group increases 8 and 6.9 times, respectively. And that of CSC-2@PEG increases about 5.8 times as primary, indicating pure CDT and starvation therapy is not enough to fight against tumor. As expected, CSC-2@PEG + NIR-II group shows prominent tumor suppression ability (Fig. 8D). After 14-day treatment, tumors almost disappear that is also confirmed by the photographs of mice and tumors as shown in Fig. 8B. Both H&E and Tunel staining tumor slices discover obvious cell damage (including: cell atrophy and nuclear necrosis) for CSC-2@PEG + NIR-II group, attributing to synergistic PTT, PDT, CDT, and starvation therapy (Fig. S16). To further evaluate the safety of above means, the tissue sections of heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney are detected by H&E staining, revealing no apparent changes in comparison with control group as shown in Fig. S17. In addition, blood biochemistry analysis also is implemented. There is obvious difference for all indicators between control and CSC-2@PEG + NIR-II group as shown in Fig. S18. With good biosafety and excellent therapeutic efficiency, CSC-2@PEG nanocomposite is expected to be a potential nanoagent for anticancer.

According to previous reports, immunogenic death (ICD) stimulates dendritic cells (DCs) maturation, which increases the number of infiltrating immune cells and triggers antitumor immune response [37]. Here, DC-maturated indicators (CD80+ CD86+) are analyzed by flow cytometry to assess the systemic immune response. From Fig. 8E, CSC-2@PEG + NIR-II group exhibits a greater percentage of mature DCs (29.8 %) in comparison with control group (8.6 %) in lymph. And the greater immune activation also can be found in tumor due to synergistic PTT/PDT/CDT/starvation therapy (Fig. S19). Moreover, the greatest up-regulation of CD11c (DCs marker) in CSC-2@PEG + NIR-II group further confirms its DCs maturation (Fig. 8F). After DCs maturation, certain complex peptide antigens are present to T cell receptors for T cell activation and to secrete various cytokines to regulate immunity [38]. The levels of cytokines such as TNF-α (mediator in cellular immunity), IL-2 (immune regulatory lymphokine in response to antigenic or mitogenic stimulation), and IFN-γ (produced from Th1 cells, CD8+ cytotoxic T and some other innate-like lymphocytes) in serum of mice are evaluated by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA). From Fig. 8G–I, CSC-2@PEG + NIR-II group exhibits 1.9 (TNF-α), 5.6 (IFN-γ), and 1.3 (IL-2) times cytokines compared with control group. It is believed that synergistic PTT/PDT/CDT and starvation therapy effectively activate immune response for anticancer.

CSCs@PEG nanocomposites are prepared to reveal the enhanced multi-enzyme mediated phototherapy. Here, a two-step hydrothermal process is adopted to synthesize novel heterostructure, which reveals great anticancer efficiency. The great performance can be ascribed as follow: 1) CSCs@PEG has high NIR-II absorption and photothermal conversion ability. Moreover, the high temperature not only introduces PTT but also increases the thermal energy of sample to fill up energy difference between excitation light (1064 nm, 1.16 eV) and band gap of Co9S8 (1.46 eV), realizing low-energy NIR-II light triggered ROS generation. 2) The nanocomposite possesses GOx-like activity which is associated with mimic POD and CAT to improve CDT and supply O2 for PDT. And the consumption of intracellular glucose also leads to starvation therapy. It is the first time for cobalt sulfide nanocomposite to be used as mimic GOx on biomedicine. 3) CSCs@PEG displays the greater ROS generation and nanozyme activity than pure Co9S8@PEG owing to efficient charge separation in heterostructure. Given the unpaired electrons in Co ion and great photothermal effect, the nanocomposite also can be used as MRI and thermal imaging contrast agent. In addition, the oxidation of sulfide into soluble sulfate dominates the biodegradation of nanostructure, facilitating elimination via feces and urine. The multi-therapy also effectively arouses immune response for anticancer.

Detail materials used, synthetic process, characterization methods, TEM images, SEM images, XRD, photothermal performance, ROS generation, XPS, histological examination, haematological analysis, UV–Vis absorption spectra, photothermal heating curves, infrared thermal images, heating/cooling experiments, thermal equilibrium time constants, (Ahν) [2]-hν curve, dispersion of nanoparticles, and hematological data.

KeKe Kang: Investigation, Writing, Visualization. Limin Wang: Investigation, Data curation. Yajie Ma: Investigation, Data curation. Kai Yu: Writing – review & editing. Jingwei Liu: Software, Methodology. Fengyu Qu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Huiming Lin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.
